did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble deep learning model combining three Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV3) for the classification of five common skin diseases (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Melanocytic Nevi, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Verruca Vulgaris). It employs confidence-based rejection to filter uncertain predictions, aiming for equitable diagnosis across different skin tones.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an ensemble deep learning model for skin disease classification. It details specific methodology (data collection from PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, and DermNet; preprocessing; model architecture using DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV3; and confidence-based rejection), and reports original results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCDS), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Ensemble Learning with Confidence-Based Rejection for Equitable Skin Disease Diagnosis Across Skin Tones
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computing and Data Science (ICCDS)
brief_summary: This study addresses algorithmic bias in skin disease diagnosis by developing an ensemble deep learning model for classifying five common conditions: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Melanocytic Nevi, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Verruca Vulgaris. The system integrates three Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV3) using hard voting and employs a confidence-based rejection mechanism with a 0.4 threshold to filter uncertain predictions. To ensure equitable performance across diverse skin tones, the model was trained on a composite dataset derived from PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, and DermNet. Evaluation results demonstrated that the ensemble approach achieved an overall accuracy of 0.81, outperforming individual base models which ranged from 0.75 to 0.76. The confidence-based rejection strategy further improved prediction reliability by discarding low-confidence samples, thereby reducing misclassifications. The authors conclude that this framework offers a robust, fair, and clinically relevant tool for dermatological diagnosis, particularly in resource-limited settings.

all_task_types.target: Five common skin disease classes: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Melanocytic Nevi, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Verruca Vulgaris.
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases using an ensemble of CNNs (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV3). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories via softmax probabilities and voting mechanisms (hard/soft voting). A confidence-based rejection mechanism filters uncertain predictions (threshold < 0.4) but does not change the primary task type from classification to regression.

datasets_used.name: Composite Dataset (PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, DermNet)
datasets_used.description: A unified composite dataset created by unifying three publicly available dermatology image datasets: PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, and DermNet. These sources were chosen to provide a heterogeneous representation of skin colors, lighting conditions, and disease types to mitigate algorithmic bias.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble deep learning model (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV3) after preprocessing (resizing to 224x224, normalization, and augmentation).
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores.
use.other: Used to ensure equitable performance across diverse skin tones by providing diverse clinical images and metadata.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To filter out uncertain predictions and reduce misclassifications by discarding samples with softmax probabilities below a threshold.
To scale confidence levels of softmax probabilities in the Weighted Soft Voting ensemble method.
To evaluate the discriminative ability of the ensemble model across the five skin disease categories.
To analyze per-class performance and identify specific misclassifications (e.g., between melanocytic nevi and seborrheic keratosis).
To evaluate the performance of individual CNN models and the ensemble model on a per-class basis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and visual analysis of ROC curves and confusion matrices.

Composite Dataset (PAD-UFES-20, Stanford.experiment: Classification of five skin diseases (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Melanocytic Nevi, Seborrheic Keratosis, Verruca Vulgaris)

Composite Dataset (PAD-UFES-20, Stanford.notes: The ensemble models outperformed individual base models. DenseNet121 was the best individual model (F1: 76.51%, Acc: 77.12%). Equal Soft Voting achieved the highest performance among ensembles (F1: 80.75%). A confidence threshold of 0.4 was used to reject uncertain predictions. ROC analysis showed AUCs > 0.9 for all classes. Melanocytic Nevi and Seborrheic Keratosis were the most challenging classes. Eczema and Verruca Vulgaris performed well with F1-scores > 0.80.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of three Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV3
architecture_used.pretraining: All three models were initialized with pre-trained ImageNet weights and subsequently fine-tuned on the in-house skin disease dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 (images were resized to this dimension during preprocessing)

architecture_details.modification: Terminal dense layers of the base models were replaced with a fully connected layer with five neurons corresponding to the five disease classes, followed by a softmax activation function to produce class probabilities.
architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Multiple voting strategies were investigated: Equal Hard Voting, Equal Soft Voting, Weighted Soft Voting (with Temperature Scaling), and Weighted Hard Voting.

hyperparameters.temperature_scaling: Used in Weighted Soft Voting to scale confidence levels based on validation set performance

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax activation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble (Weighted Soft Voting with Temperature Scaling)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization using ImageNet mean and standard deviations
conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep learning model, combining DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV3, achieved an overall accuracy of 0.81 and an F1-score of 0.8075 (Equal Soft Voting) on the test set, outperforming individual base models which ranged from 0.75 to 0.76 accuracy.

The integration of a confidence-based rejection mechanism with a threshold of 0.4 effectively filtered out uncertain predictions, reducing misclassifications and enhancing the reliability of the remaining diagnoses.
The Weighted Soft Voting ensemble with Temperature Scaling provided an optimal trade-off between performance and reliability, maintaining an F1-score of 0.8012 while rejecting only 28 samples.
The model demonstrated strong discriminative ability across all five skin disease classes, with Area Under the Curve (AUC) values greater than 0.9 for all categories.

Eczema and Verruca Vulgaris were classified with high performance (F1-scores > 0.80), whereas Melanocytic Nevi and Seborrheic Keratosis remained challenging classes with frequent confusion between them.

Using a composite dataset from PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, and DermNet helped mitigate algorithmic bias by providing diverse skin tone representation, leading to equitable performance across different populations.

The system is positioned as a robust, fair, and clinically relevant auxiliary tool for dermatological diagnosis, particularly suitable for resource-limited settings where specialist expertise may be scarce.
Future work will focus on real-time deployment, improving interpretability through methods like saliency maps or Grad-CAM, and expanding datasets to include rarer conditions.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only five common skin diseases (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Melanocytic Nevi, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Verruca Vulgaris), excluding rarer or more complex conditions, which limits generalizability.

The model relies on a composite dataset derived from PAD-UFES-20, Stanford AIMI, and DermNet; while this aims to mitigate bias, the paper does not report performance stratified by specific skin tones (e.g., Fitzpatrick scale), leaving the actual equity claims unverified by quantitative subgroup metrics.

The ensemble model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.81, which, while superior to individual base models (0.75-0.76), is not exceptionally high for clinical diagnostic tools, suggesting potential limitations in real-world robustness.

Significant confusion exists between Melanocytic Nevi and Seborrheic Keratosis, with lower F1-scores (0.74-0.77) for these classes compared to others, indicating a specific diagnostic weakness in the model.

The confidence-based rejection mechanism (threshold 0.4) discards uncertain predictions rather than correcting them; this reduces the total number of available diagnoses and requires human review, impacting clinical workflow efficiency.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the ensemble and base models, relying instead on descriptive statistics.

No external validation on a completely independent dataset was performed; the model was evaluated only on the test split of the composite dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific data sources used.

The study is a conference paper (ICCDS 2025) with a relatively small total sample size (1039 images), which may limit the statistical power and generalizability of the findings compared to large-scale clinical trials.

Deployment concerns are acknowledged by the authors, who note that future work is needed for real-time deployment and interpretability (e.g., saliency maps), indicating the current model lacks explainability features required for high-stakes clinical trust.
